perturbation.original_text: Article 2 – Obligation to keep Confidential and Restrictive Use The Member shall: a) not disclose any Confidential Information or Proprie tary Information unless expressly authorised by the ICC Court; b) use any Confidential Information or Proprietary Information exclusively for the purpose of its collaboration with the ICC Court and not for its own or anyone else’s benefit; c) keep secu re, confidential and hold all Confidential Information and Proprietary Information with no less a degree of care as is used for the Member’s own confidential or Proprietary Information and at least with reasonable care; and d) not amend, vary, develop, im prove, enhance (“Derivation”) the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information except as otherwise approved in writing by the ICC Court. In addition, ICC Court members are only permitted to disclose Confidential information or Proprietary informati on for the sole purpose of conflict checks. They must only disclose such information to the person duly authorized to run conflict checks within their respective law firm or company. ICC Court members undertake that their firm or company will not use the C onfidential information or Proprietary information for any other purpose and remain responsible for ensuring that such information is immediately destroyed upon conclusion of the conflict check.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 2 – Obligation to keep Confidential and Restrictive Use The Member shall: a) not disclose any Protected Data or Proprie tary Information unless expressly authorised by the ICC Court; b) use any Protected Data or Proprietary Information exclusively for the purpose of its collaboration with the ICC Court and not for its own or anyone else’s benefit; c) keep secu re, confidential and hold all Protected Data and Proprietary Information with no less a degree of care as is used for the Member’s own confidential or Proprietary Information and at least with reasonable care; and d) not amend, vary, develop, im prove, enhance (“Derivation”) the Protected Data or Proprietary Information except as otherwise approved in writing by the ICC Court. In addition, ICC Court members are only permitted to disclose Protected Data or Proprietary informati on for the sole purpose of conflict checks. They must only disclose such information to the person duly authorized to run conflict checks within their respective law firm or company. ICC Court members undertake that their firm or company will not use the C onfidential information or Proprietary information for any other purpose and remain responsible for ensuring that such information is immediately destroyed upon conclusion of the conflict check.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Protected Data' in the first part of Article 2, while maintaining 'Confidential information' in the last part about conflict checks, the agreement creates ambiguity. It's unclear whether the conflict check exception applies only to what was previously defined as Confidential Information or also extends to the newly termed 'Protected Data'. Since 'Protected Data' is undefined, it's uncertain what information is subject to the conflict check procedure. This leads to an in-text contradiction in Article 2 about how those two terms should be treated in relation to conflict checks.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Article 2 – Obligation to keep Confidential and Restrictive Use The Member shall: a) not disclose any Protected Data or Proprie tary Information unless expressly authorised by the ICC Court; b) use any Protected Data or Proprietary Information exclusively for the purpose of its collaboration with the ICC Court and not for its own or anyone else’s benefit; c) keep secu re, confidential and hold all Protected Data and Proprietary Information with no less a degree of care as is used for the Member’s own confidential or Proprietary Information and at least with reasonable care; and d) not amend, vary, develop, im prove, enhance (“Derivation”) the Protected Data or Proprietary Information except as otherwise approved in writing by the ICC Court. In addition, ICC Court members are only permitted to disclose Confidential information or Proprietary informati on for the sole purpose of conflict checks. They must only disclose such information to the person duly authorized to run conflict checks within their respective law firm or company. ICC Court members undertake that their firm or company will not use the C onfidential information or Proprietary information for any other purpose and remain responsible for ensuring that such information is immediately destroyed upon conclusion of the conflict check.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Protected Data' in the first part of Article 2, while keeping 'Confidential information' in the last part regarding conflict checks. The contradicted text is exactly the same as the changed text. The change introduces an ambiguity as to whether conflict checks apply only to what was previously defined as Confidential Information or to the newly introduced 'Protected Data'. Since 'Protected Data' is undefined, it's unclear what information is subject to the conflict check procedure. This creates a contradiction within Article 2 about how these two terms should be treated in relation to conflict checks, meeting the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Article 7 – No Warranty The ICC Court makes available the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information as is and only warrants that, it has done its best efforts to ensure to the best possible extent that the information disclosed is complete, accurate, free from defects or third party rights, and useful for the purposes of the Member.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 7 – No Warranty The ICC Court makes available the Private Data or Proprietary Information as is and only warrants that, it has done its best efforts to ensure to the best possible extent that the information disclosed is complete, accurate, free from defects or third party rights, and useful for the purposes of the Member.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Private Data' in Article 7, while not defining 'Private Data' anywhere in the agreement, the document now provides a warranty regarding the 'Private Data' without clarifying what 'Private Data' encompasses. This leads to confusion because Article 1 defines Confidential Information, yet Article 7 warranties 'Private Data', which is nowhere defined. This creates an in-text contradiction, as the reader does not know what "Private Data" relates to or where it starts, and it relates to the warranty, leading to ambiguity for the Member.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Article 7 – No Warranty The ICC Court makes available the Private Data or Proprietary Information as is and only warrants that, it has done its best efforts to ensure to the best possible extent that the information disclosed is complete, accurate, free from defects or third party rights, and useful for the purposes of the Member.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Private Data'. The original text defines 'Confidential Information', whereas the changed text uses 'Private Data', which is not defined anywhere. Because 'Private Data' is nowhere defined and the warranty hinges on the information disclosed, a clear contradiction now exists. The contradicted text is actually the changed text itself, so the warranty refers to Private Data without clarity.

perturbation.original_text: Article 10 – Survival of Obligations Upon termination of this Agreement, the Member shall stop making us e of Confidential Information or Proprietary Information. The Parties’ obligations under this Agreement shall survive indefinitely or to the longest extent permitted by the applicable laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 10 – Survival of Obligations Upon termination of this Agreement, the Member shall stop making us e of Sensitive Information or Proprietary Information. The Parties’ obligations under this Agreement shall survive indefinitely or to the longest extent permitted by the applicable laws.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Sensitive Information' in Article 10, without defining 'Sensitive Information', there's ambiguity about what obligations survive the termination of the agreement. 'Confidential Information' is defined in Article 1, setting a clear standard for what the Member must protect. 'Sensitive Information' lacks a definition, so the scope of the surviving obligations is unclear, creating a contradiction, because it is not sure to the member, what to do. The ambiguity arises within Article 10, as the text refers to 'obligations under this agreement,' which relate back to Article 1 and its definition of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Article 10 – Survival of Obligations Upon termination of this Agreement, the Member shall stop making us e of Sensitive Information or Proprietary Information. The Parties’ obligations under this Agreement shall survive indefinitely or to the longest extent permitted by the applicable laws.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Sensitive Information' in Article 10. Since 'Sensitive Information' is not defined anywhere in the contract, unlike 'Confidential Information' which is defined in Article 1, the scope of the obligations that survive termination becomes unclear. This creates a direct conflict because the contradicted text (which is the same as the changed text) now refers to an undefined term, making it impossible to determine what obligations the member has upon termination, leading to an in-text contradiction, and following the Misaligned Terminology definition.

perturbation.original_text: Article 11 – Breach and Remedies The Parties agree that Confid ential Information or Proprietary Information may relate to highly sensitive aspects of ICC Court’s activities or arbitral proceedings and that loss, misuse or unauthorised Derivation or disclosure of the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information may be highly prejudicial to the interests of ICC and the ICC Court, and that financial compensation may not adequatly compensate ICC for any such damage. ICC therefore reserves the right to take whatever injunctive or other action it deems necessary, at law, in equity or otherwise to protect its interests in the event of any actual and/or alleged breach of this Agreement by the Member.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 11 – Breach and Remedies The Parties agree that Restricted Data or Proprietary Information may relate to highly sensitive aspects of ICC Court’s activities or arbitral proceedings and that loss, misuse or unauthorised Derivation or disclosure of the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information may be highly prejudicial to the interests of ICC and the ICC Court, and that financial compensation may not adequatly compensate ICC for any such damage. ICC therefore reserves the right to take whatever injunctive or other action it deems necessary, at law, in equity or otherwise to protect its interests in the event of any actual and/or alleged breach of this Agreement by the Member.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Restricted Data' in the first part of Article 11, while keeping 'Confidential Information' in the second half creates an in-text contradiction regarding breach and remedies. Is a breach relating to Restricted Data also considered a breach covered by this article? The article discusses the harm caused by loss of 'Confidential Information,' but if the breach involves 'Restricted Data', it is unclear whether the remedies apply or not, or if the term is also included. This ambiguity weakens the enforceability of this clause.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Article 11 – Breach and Remedies The Parties agree that Restricted Data or Proprietary Information may relate to highly sensitive aspects of ICC Court’s activities or arbitral proceedings and that loss, misuse or unauthorised Derivation or disclosure of the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information may be highly prejudicial to the interests of ICC and the ICC Court, and that financial compensation may not adequatly compensate ICC for any such damage. ICC therefore reserves the right to take whatever injunctive or other action it deems necessary, at law, in equity or otherwise to protect its interests in the event of any actual and/or alleged breach of this Agreement by the Member.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Restricted Data' in the first part of Article 11 while retaining 'Confidential Information' later in the same article creates a direct conflict. The Contradicted Text, which is identical to the Changed Text, highlights this issue. The explanation correctly points out that it's now unclear whether remedies apply to breaches involving 'Restricted Data', leading to ambiguity and weakening enforceability. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition, where multiple terms are used interchangeably without clear definition, causing conflicting obligations within the contract.

perturbation.original_text: Article 12 – Disposal Within ninety (90) days of termination of this Agreement, the ICC Court may request the disposal of the Confidential Information or Proprietary Information disclosed to the Member. Disposal means execution of reasonable measures to return or destroy all materials and copies including electronic data. Destruction shall be confirmed in writing. Disposa l shall be effected within thirty (30) days of the request being made.
perturbation.changed_text: Article 12 – Disposal Within ninety (90) days of termination of this Agreement, the ICC Court may request the disposal of the Case Information or Proprietary Information disclosed to the Member. Disposal means execution of reasonable measures to return or destroy all materials and copies including electronic data. Destruction shall be confirmed in writing. Disposa l shall be effected within thirty (30) days of the request being made.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Case Information' in Article 12, the agreement introduces a new term without defining it. The obligation to dispose of 'Case Information' is now unclear since it is undefined, does it only include case details? While 'Confidential Information' has a definition, 'Case Information' does not, leading to uncertainty. This creates an internal contradiction, because is not sure what materials need to be destroyed under “Case Information”, that contrasts with the previously known “Confidential Information”. This ambiguity could render the disposal clause unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Article 12 – Disposal Within ninety (90) days of termination of this Agreement, the ICC Court may request the disposal of the Case Information or Proprietary Information disclosed to the Member. Disposal means execution of reasonable measures to return or destroy all materials and copies including electronic data. Destruction shall be confirmed in writing. Disposa l shall be effected within thirty (30) days of the request being made.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Case Information' in Article 12. The explanation states this introduces a new undefined term, 'Case Information,' which contrasts with the previously defined 'Confidential Information,' creating uncertainty about what materials need to be disposed of. This uncertainty directly conflicts with the purpose of the disposal clause, creating a strong in-text contradiction and rendering the clause potentially unenforceable. The contradicted text is the changed text itself, since the change causes a conflict.
